constructs: need-for-certainty motivation categorization efficiency social structure
claim: Desire to categorize people efficiently and maintain psychological certainty, not prejudice, drives negative evaluation of stereotype-violating individuals.

phenomenon: environmental cues override categorical cues in stereotype formation
claim: Stereotypes primarily reflect inferred behavioral ecology rather than race; when environment information is available, perceivers weight it more heavily than categorical identity.

phenomenon: memory of moral transgressions fades preferentially
claim: Memories of unethical actions are obfuscated over time because they threaten moral self-image; this motivated forgetting enables repeated dishonesty.

phenomenon: mindset intervention reduces formal discipline rates
claim: Shifting teachers from punitive to empathic-understanding mindsets via brief online intervention halves suspension rates by improving teacher-student relationships.
Uncalculating cooperation, especially when observable, serves as an honest signal of future trustworthiness beyond efficiency gains.

phenomenon: fairness salience differs pre and post allocation
claim: Allocators mispredict receiver satisfaction because they inhabit a pre-choice perspective where procedure differences loom large, while receivers inhabit a post-choice perspective where outcome magnitude dominates.
